article_meta.title: Once upon a time, school students would submit an essay, and teachers would mark it

reasoning: The model exhibits several failure patterns: (1) It flags quoted text as bias in item 2 (attribution rule violation - the quotes from O'Rourke are inside quotation marks), (2) It uses bias type labels not from the defined list ('Selection bias', 'Framing bias', 'Omission bias', 'Authority bias' are not valid types in the provided taxonomy), (3) Item 4 flags attributed source language (Catt's statement) as bias. The model does identify some defensible author-level concerns (item 1 about story choice/selection of the school, item 3 about omission of drawbacks), but applies wrong labels. It misses some potential bias like the generally positive/promotional tone of the author's prose and the word 'innovative' used multiple times.

custom_reasoning.attribution_rule_compliance: Item 2 explicitly flags text inside quotation marks (O'Rourke's quotes) as bias. Item 4 flags attributed speech from Graham Catt. These are clear attribution rule violations. Two out of four items violate the rule.
custom_reasoning.false_positive_rate: Items 2 and 4 are attribution violations and thus false positives. Item 1 has some merit as a story choice/slant observation but is somewhat overreaching. Item 3 is a defensible omission observation in the author's own prose.
custom_reasoning.false_negative_rate: The model missed the author's repeated use of 'innovative' (used twice - 'innovative ways'), the generally promotional tone of the author's connecting prose, and the author's positive framing language like 'brand-new tech to further learning.' These are moderate misses.
custom_reasoning.bias_type_accuracy: None of the labels used ('Selection bias', 'Framing bias', 'Omission bias', 'Authority bias') appear in the defined list of valid bias types. The correct labels would have been 'Bias by Story Choice and Placement', 'Slant', 'Bias by Omission', and 'Elite vs. Populist Bias' or 'Slant' respectively. All four labels are wrong.
